does: This paper develops a certified unlearning algorithm for minimax optimization problems, extending prior work that primarily addressed single-variable statistical models. The core contribution is a minimax unlearning step combining a total-Hessian-based complete Newton update with the Gaussian mechanism from differential privacy. The method calibrates Gaussian noise injection by analyzing the sensitivity of the unlearning step—specifically, how closely the unlearned variables approximate those from retraining from scratch. The paper derives (ε,δ)-certified privacy guarantees and establishes generalization rates for population strong and weak primal-dual risk across three loss function categories: convex-concave, strongly-convex-concave, and other variants. It also provides deletion capacity bounds indicating how many samples can be removed while maintaining desired population risk.
connects: This work extends the certified machine unlearning literature, which has grown around providing formal privacy guarantees for data deletion in trained models. Prior unlearning methods relied on Hessian-based Newton updates suitable only for convex objectives with single parameters, making them inapplicable to minimax settings common in adversarial training, GANs, and game-theoretic learning. The paper builds on differential privacy's Gaussian mechanism framework and combines it with minimax optimization theory, bridging two previously separate research directions. It engages with recent advances in understanding minimax learning dynamics and differential privacy's sensitivity analysis, adapting these tools to the dual-variable structure inherent in minimax problems.
enables: This framework opens avenues for certified unlearning in adversarial and game-theoretic learning settings where minimax formulations are fundamental. Applications include privacy-aware adversarial training, GAN development with deletion guarantees, and fairness-machine learning systems with formal unlearning certificates. The deletion capacity bounds suggest practical guidelines for managing dataset size while preserving model performance. Future work could extend these results to non-convex minimax problems relevant to deep learning, develop more efficient unlearning algorithms reducing the computational overhead of total-Hessian computations, and apply certified unlearning to multi-agent learning scenarios. The theoretical framework also provides foundations for studying privacy-utility tradeoffs in competitive learning settings.
